hypothesis: Replacing the deterministic FIFO oracle in SOTSS-MIN with a stochastic Binomial oracle (same seed as evaluation) allows detection of spot-interruption-vulnerable ticks missed by the deterministic oracle, yielding a lower-cost schedule with equal or better safety.
finding: SOTSS-GSF produces identical c_schedules to SOTSS-MIN on both traces. The stochastic oracle degenerates to the deterministic oracle at p_interrupt=10%/hr because p_survive per 60s tick = (0.90)^(1/60) ~ 0.9982. Binomial(c_spot, 0.9982) almost always returns c_spot, so c_effective ~ c in every oracle iteration. The oracle fix-up loop therefore sees the same queue dynamics as the deterministic oracle and converges to the same schedule.
root_cause: Low per-tick interruption probability. At p_interrupt=10%/hr with 60s ticks, each spot instance survives with probability ~99.82%. The expected number of interruptions per tick per spot instance is ~0.18%, so across the full trace the stochastic oracle almost never sees a c_effective reduction. The hypothesis requires per-tick p_survive << 1 to create meaningfully different oracle views between seeds or methods.
azure_verdict: NEUTRAL — SOTSS-GSF == SOTSS-MIN. Azure frontier unchanged at 160,107 goodput/$.
burstgpt_verdict: UNSAFE — SOTSS-GSF (gate=100%) is 4 requests short of AMCSG safety criterion (n_sla_safe=5860 < baseline=5864). Note: this is the oracle-simulation gap: oracle uses simplified stochastic FIFO (c_effective then deterministic queue), evaluator uses full stochastic FIFO simulation. Same gap exists for SOTSS-MIN gate=100% on BurstGPT. BurstGPT frontier remains SOTSS gate=20% at 170,572.
